aliases: Black Urad Dal with Kidney Beans

english: Black Urad Dal with Kidney Beans

ontology.foodex2_description: Lentil-based dish, cooked with butter and cream (dal makhani type)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for dal makhani; A0CKP (cooked legume dish) used as closest defensible analogue. Verified against FoodEx2 browser as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dal makhani as a specific food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of dal makhani is approximately 300 g, providing around 550 kcal, 20 g protein, 45 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dal makhani nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using whole black gram (urad dal) and kidney bean (rajma) cooked dish entries, adjusted for the high butter and cream content typical of restaurant-style dal makhani. Input per_100g values (energy 183 kcal, protein 6.67 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 6.67 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN cooked lentil dish ranges adjusted for added dairy fats. Sodium 266.67 mg/100g retained from input as plausible for a restaurant preparation. Potassium estimated at 260 mg/100g from ICMR-NIN urad dal and rajma cooked values. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal makhani GI estimated using whole black gram (urad dal) GI of approximately 29 (low GI) as the primary legume base, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked whole black lentils and kidney beans (rajma GI ~29). The high fat content from butter and cream further attenuates glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (29) × carbohydrate per serving (6.67 g/100g × 300 g = 20 g) / 100 = 5.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100g (borderline) and saturated fat 8.33 g/100g (high)
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 150 g to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per serving, and request reduced butter and cream to lower saturated fat, which contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (29) from black lentils and kidney beans, but high saturated fat (8.33 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal makhani has a low glycaemic index of approximately 29 due to its legume base; however, the high saturated fat from butter and cream can impair insulin sensitivity, so portion control to 150–200 g and pairing with a high-fibre whole-grain bread is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33 g/100g and cholesterol 33.33 mg/100g from butter, cream, and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of 8.33 g per 100 g significantly exceeds the 5 g/100g cardiovascular threshold; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (100–150 g) and request a reduced-dairy preparation to lower LDL cholesterol risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33 g/100g from animal dairy fats exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 8.33 g/100g from butter, ghee, and cream can trigger TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish unless prepared with substantially reduced dairy fats, and should monitor nightshade sensitivity from tomatoes and chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Makhani is a rich, creamy North Indian lentil dish originating from the Punjab region of India, widely regarded as one of the most iconic dishes of Punjabi cuisine. It is made primarily from whole black lentils (urad dal) and red kidney beans (rajma), slow-cooked overnight — traditionally on a tandoor or low wood fire — until the lentils are completely tender and the starches have broken down into a thick, velvety gravy. The dish is finished with generous quantities of butter and cream, which give it its characteristic silky texture and deep, indulgent flavour. A tempering (tadka) of cumin, garlic, ginger, onions, tomatoes, and a blend of spices including garam masala, coriander, chilli powder, and dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi) is incorporated during cooking. The 'Khaas' (special) restaurant version often includes a house masala blend and may be enriched further with paneer or cashew paste. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 183 kcal, 6.67 g protein, 15 g fat (of which 8.33 g is saturated fat from dairy), and 6.67 g carbohydrate. The glycaemic index is low (approximately 29) due to the legume base, but the high saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. Sodium at 266.67 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a main course with naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice in Indian restaurants across Singapore and globally.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked lentil dish entries adjusted for high restaurant-style dairy fat content. The input values from the GrabFood listing are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for this preparation style. No exact published nutrient analysis for 'Dal Makhani Khaas' from an approved primary source was available; values represent a well-supported adaptation. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous legume entries.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for restaurant-style dal makhani is not available in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values for the specific restaurant preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. Potassium estimated at 260 mg/100g from ICMR-NIN cooked urad dal and rajma values. Input portions contained three duplicate entries for '1 bowl / 300 g'; deduplicated to a single default serving of 300 g. The 'Khaas' (special/chef recommendation) designation refers to a restaurant-specific preparation at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore, and does not alter the base nutritional profile materially beyond the standard dal makhani preparation. Ethnic tags 'Biryani' and 'Butter Chicken' in the original input appear to be restaurant category tags rather than dish descriptors and have been excluded from this record.
